response:
I read ISSUE-41.md and traced the execution-design call against the advisor table. Since running the `adr-lint.mjs` command itself required approval that wasn't granted in this session, I manually verified the new ADR against the lint script's actual rules (frontmatter, id/filename match, no version strings, no dangling `ADR NNNN` cites, a `[checkable-doc]`/`[unverifiable]+REOPEN-IF` criterion present, char budget) rather than re-attempting the blocked command.

**Decision: O4 — pre-registered sequential escalation.** Recorded as `docs/decisions/0035-tiered-agent-test-execution-escalation.md` (5,399 chars, under the 6,000 cap).

Rationale in brief:
- Run all 4 arms × 8 tasks at the pre-registered **3-rep floor** (Method, ISSUE-41.md:42-43) as the base — "minimal reps first" means this floor, not fewer.
- Then apply the Analysis-section clause (line 49): escalate reps/tasks only on (task, arm) cells whose CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous.
- All 3 advisors converged unanimously at 5/5/5 — O4 isn't a competing option, it's Comment 3's own already-agreed method (line 70), sized to the ~250-300-invocation estimate (line 71).
- Rejected O1 (full grid, no checkpoint) — forgoes the line-49 escalation checkpoint entirely, overspending on already-unambiguous cells.
- Rejected O2/O3 (single-arm pilots) — both are unregistered arm-level stopping rules: O2 forecloses the mandatory Opus-quality "real prize" report; O3 never runs the Tiered arm at all and can't resolve the actual decision gate (Tiered vs Sonnet-solo). Both scored low (2s/1s) as exactly the fishing risk pre-registration exists to prevent.

Open revisit trigger worth flagging when the run starts: if the first escalation checkpoint shows 3-rep CIs are degenerately wide, the starting floor itself needs raising, not just the increment (Advisor 2's reversal premise) — captured as an `[unverifiable]` in the ADR.

options.label: Full pre-registered grid
options.summary: Run all 4 arms (Haiku-solo, Sonnet-solo, Opus-solo, Tiered) across all 8 pre-screened tasks at 3 reps each before any analysis.

options.label: Pilot Tiered arm first
options.summary: Run Tiered vs Sonnet-solo baseline first at pre-registered reps; check the delta/token decision rule; stop before scaling other arms if already decidable.

options.label: Pilot Opus-solo first
options.summary: Run the costliest-per-invocation arm, Opus-solo, plus Sonnet-solo baseline first to test reachability of the 'Opus-quality at sub-Opus-cost' prize before full-grid spend.

options.label: Pre-registered sequential escalation
options.summary: Run all 4 arms at minimal reps first, adding reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary, per Comment 1's stated escalation method.

O1.crux: Matches Comment 1's fixed 'Method (lean)' base (4 arms x 8 tasks x 3 reps) exactly, but running it wholesale 'before any analysis' forgoes the escalation clause the pre-registration itself specifies, so it can overspend on tasks/arms that are already unambiguous.
O1.dependency: False if: Comment 1's 'sequential escalation' is read as a post-hoc add-on to the fixed 3-rep base rather than a replacement for it — then the 3-rep grid IS the whole registered design and O1 is the fully correct execution, not merely a safe-but-inefficient one.

O2.crux: Stopping after only Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo forecloses ever running Haiku-solo/Opus-solo, so the pre-registered requirement to 'also report whether Tiered reaches Opus-solo quality at sub-Opus cost (the real prize)' becomes unanswerable — this is an unregistered stopping rule invoked precisely to avoid the spend the pre-reg calls for, which is the fishing risk Comment 1 says pre-registration exists to prevent.
O2.dependency: False if the owner formally amends the pre-registration to decouple the go/no-go decision rule (delta/tokens vs Sonnet-solo) from the 'real prize' Opus-quality report, making the latter optional/deferred rather than a required output of this experiment — then gating further spend on the Tiered-vs-Sonnet delta alone is defensible and the score should rise.

O3.crux: Opus-solo-vs-Sonnet-solo needs no new code and could cheaply test prize-reachability before funding the tiered-orchestration script (Comment 3's 'new code needed'), but it never runs the Tiered arm, so it cannot make the actual pre-registered decision gate (keyed on Tiered vs Sonnet-solo) 'decidable' — it answers a different, adjacent question.
O3.dependency: False if the real near-term goal is a feasibility/prerequisite gate — 'is Opus-quality reachable by any means at all' — used to decide whether to write the tiered-orchestration script in the first place, distinct from the pre-registered adoption decision; under that framing O3 is a sensible cheap upfront filter and should score higher (~4).

O4.crux: Reproduces Comment 1's own words almost verbatim ('add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous') and keeps all 4 arms in play throughout, so it can both answer the pre-registered decision rule and the Opus-quality 'real prize' question while spending less than the full grid on already-unambiguous cells — this is the pre-registration's stated method, not a deviation from it.
O4.dependency: False if 'minimal reps first' is a mischaracterization of Comment 1 — i.e., if the pre-reg's 3-replicate count is itself the non-negotiable floor and 'sequential escalation' only ever adds reps beyond 3 (never starts below it) — then O4 as worded understates the registered base and collapses back into a (less precisely stated) version of O1.

O1.crux: Matches the pre-registered 3-reps x 4-arms x 8-tasks base method, but running it 'before any analysis' skips the escalation checkpoint Comment 1 built in for ambiguous CIs.
O1.dependency: If the escalation clause only means 'may add more later' rather than 'must checkpoint after the base grid,' O1 is equivalent to O4 and should score as high.

O2.crux: Piloting Tiered vs Sonnet-solo can decide the primary adoption rule cheaply, but stopping there abandons the mandatory 'Opus-quality at sub-Opus-cost' report and the 4-arm cost/quality frontier Comment 1 commits to.
O2.dependency: If that secondary Opus-prize report is optional/deferrable rather than a committed pre-registered deliverable, this pilot-and-stop is legitimate and lean.

O3.crux: Piloting Opus-solo+Sonnet-solo never runs the Tiered arm, so it cannot inform the primary pre-registered hypothesis (Tiered vs Sonnet-solo) at all.
O3.dependency: If the 'real prize' (Opus-quality at sub-Opus cost) were actually the primary registered hypothesis instead of a secondary report, front-loading Opus-solo would be the right call.

O4.crux: This is a direct restatement of Comment 1's own analysis method ('add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous') applied across all 4 arms, so it stays lean without sacrificing the frontier/prize check.
O4.dependency: If minimal (3) reps per arm can't yield a usable CI at all (e.g., degenerate variance), the escalation procedure needs a higher starting floor, undermining this option's feasibility as described.

O1.crux: Matches the literal pre-registered baseline (4 arms x 8 tasks x 3 reps, ISSUE-41.md:33-43) and is safe, but ignores the escalation control Comment 1 itself specifies for cost.
O1.dependency: If 'sequential escalation' (line 49) means the registered plan starts at fewer than 3 reps and only grows the sample, not that 3 reps is a fixed floor to run in full before any check, then O1 wastes exactly the spend the pre-registration built escalation to avoid.

O2.crux: Arm-level early stopping after only Tiered-vs-Sonnet isn't the pre-registered escalation (which scales reps/tasks within the full 4-arm design, not which arms run at all) and risks the 'fishing for a favorable cut' the pre-registration was written to prevent (line 27).
O2.dependency: If the primary adoption decision rule (line 51, Tiered vs Sonnet-solo delta+tokens) is the only thing that matters and the 'real prize'/frontier reporting (line 51, line 48) is genuinely optional, arm-level early stopping is legitimate and the score should be higher.

O3.crux: Piloting Opus-solo (plus Sonnet-solo) can't resolve the pre-registered decision gate at all, since that gate is defined on Tiered vs Sonnet-solo (line 51), not on Opus-solo reachability — it front-loads the costliest arm without testing the thing the gate actually decides.
O3.dependency: If the Opus-ceiling question ('Opus-quality at sub-Opus cost', line 51 and the Idea section's Opus framing) is actually the load-bearing motivation for the whole issue and a cheap Opus-solo pilot could kill the project before any Tiered spend, front-loading it is the correct risk-reduction move and the score should be much higher.

O4.crux: This is the design Comment 3 explicitly cites as already agreed ('per Comment 1's stated escalation method', line 70), so it's not really a competing option — it's the pre-registered plan, and it controls the ~250-300 invocation cost (line 71) without any arm-level fishing.
O4.dependency: If Comment 1's 3-reps-per-cell figure (line 43) is the mandatory starting floor rather than a ceiling reachable via escalation, O4's 'minimal reps first' framing understates the registered starting sample and would itself be a deviation from pre-registration.

O1.crux: Matches Comment 1's fixed 'Method (lean)' base (4 arms x 8 tasks x 3 reps) exactly, but running it wholesale 'before any analysis' forgoes the escalation clause the pre-registration itself specifies, so it can overspend on tasks/arms that are already unambiguous.
O1.dependency: False if: Comment 1's 'sequential escalation' is read as a post-hoc add-on to the fixed 3-rep base rather than a replacement for it — then the 3-rep grid IS the whole registered design and O1 is the fully correct execution, not merely a safe-but-inefficient one.

O2.crux: Stopping after only Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo forecloses ever running Haiku-solo/Opus-solo, so the pre-registered requirement to 'also report whether Tiered reaches Opus-solo quality at sub-Opus cost (the real prize)' becomes unanswerable — this is an unregistered stopping rule invoked precisely to avoid the spend the pre-reg calls for, which is the fishing risk Comment 1 says pre-registration exists to prevent.
O2.dependency: False if the owner formally amends the pre-registration to decouple the go/no-go decision rule (delta/tokens vs Sonnet-solo) from the 'real prize' Opus-quality report, making the latter optional/deferred rather than a required output of this experiment — then gating further spend on the Tiered-vs-Sonnet delta alone is defensible and the score should rise.

O3.crux: Opus-solo-vs-Sonnet-solo needs no new code and could cheaply test prize-reachability before funding the tiered-orchestration script (Comment 3's 'new code needed'), but it never runs the Tiered arm, so it cannot make the actual pre-registered decision gate (keyed on Tiered vs Sonnet-solo) 'decidable' — it answers a different, adjacent question.
O3.dependency: False if the real near-term goal is a feasibility/prerequisite gate — 'is Opus-quality reachable by any means at all' — used to decide whether to write the tiered-orchestration script in the first place, distinct from the pre-registered adoption decision; under that framing O3 is a sensible cheap upfront filter and should score higher (~4).

O4.crux: Reproduces Comment 1's own words almost verbatim ('add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous') and keeps all 4 arms in play throughout, so it can both answer the pre-registered decision rule and the Opus-quality 'real prize' question while spending less than the full grid on already-unambiguous cells — this is the pre-registration's stated method, not a deviation from it.
O4.dependency: False if 'minimal reps first' is a mischaracterization of Comment 1 — i.e., if the pre-reg's 3-replicate count is itself the non-negotiable floor and 'sequential escalation' only ever adds reps beyond 3 (never starts below it) — then O4 as worded understates the registered base and collapses back into a (less precisely stated) version of O1.

O1.crux: Matches the pre-registered 3-reps x 4-arms x 8-tasks base method, but running it 'before any analysis' skips the escalation checkpoint Comment 1 built in for ambiguous CIs.
O1.dependency: If the escalation clause only means 'may add more later' rather than 'must checkpoint after the base grid,' O1 is equivalent to O4 and should score as high.

O2.crux: Piloting Tiered vs Sonnet-solo can decide the primary adoption rule cheaply, but stopping there abandons the mandatory 'Opus-quality at sub-Opus-cost' report and the 4-arm cost/quality frontier Comment 1 commits to.
O2.dependency: If that secondary Opus-prize report is optional/deferrable rather than a committed pre-registered deliverable, this pilot-and-stop is legitimate and lean.

O3.crux: Piloting Opus-solo+Sonnet-solo never runs the Tiered arm, so it cannot inform the primary pre-registered hypothesis (Tiered vs Sonnet-solo) at all.

artifacts.docs/decisions/0035-tiered-agent-test-ex:
---
id: 0035
title: "Issue #41 tiered-agent test: execute as pre-registered sequential escalation, not full-grid-first or arm-level pilot"
status: accepted
summary: "Run the tiered-agent empirical test (issue #41) as Comment 3's already-agreed design: all 4 arms (Haiku-solo/Sonnet-solo/Opus-solo/Tiered) at the pre-registered 3-rep/8-task base, THEN escalate reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary (Comment 1, ISSUE-41.md:49), stopping when unambiguous. Rejects running the full grid with no checkpoint (wastes spend on already-unambiguous cells) and rejects piloting a single arm first (Tiered-only or Opus-only) as an unregistered stopping rule that forecloses the mandatory Opus-quality 'real prize' report and can't resolve the actual decision gate."
---

# 0035 — issue #41 execution design: escalation, not full-grid-first or single-arm pilot

- Date: 2026-07-14
- Owner: PM
- Panel: 3 independent advisors, round 1 + anonymized-reveal round 2 where estimates diverged, scoring O1 (full grid, no checkpoint), O2 (pilot Tiered-only), O3 (pilot Opus-only), O4 (pre-registered sequential escalation).
- Context: ISSUE-41.md's pre-registration (Comment 1, lines 25-59) sets a 4-arm x 8-task x 3-rep base (lines 33-43) AND a sequential-escalation clause under Analysis ("add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous", line 49). Comment 3 (lines 65-71) already cites this escalation method as the agreed execution design and estimates ~250-300 invocations on that basis. The open question this ADR settles: when the experiment runs, execute the full grid unconditionally, or run the base grid then checkpoint per line 49?

## Decision
Execute **O4**: run all 4 arms across all 8 pre-screened tasks at the pre-registered 3-rep floor (Method, ISSUE-41.md:42-43) as the base — "minimal reps first" means this 3-rep floor, not fewer; escalation ADDS beyond it, it does not replace it. Then apply the Analysis-section escalation clause (line 49): add reps/tasks only to (task, arm) cells whose CI straddles the decision boundary; stop escalating once unambiguous. Keep all 4 arms in the design throughout — no arm-level pilot-and-stop.

## Justification
All 3 advisors converged unanimously on O4 at the top score (5/5/5, no spread, no round-2 needed): it is not a competing option but Comment 3's own already-cited agreed method (line 70), sized against the ~250-300-invocation estimate (line 71), and it is the only option that both answers the primary decision rule (Tiered vs Sonnet-solo, line 51) and keeps the mandatory Opus-quality "real prize" report (line 51) live, while avoiding spend on cells the pre-registration itself built escalation to skip. O1 scored close (4/3/4) but forgoes the line-49 checkpoint entirely ("before any analysis" is the crux each advisor flagged) — safe but wasteful. O2 and O3 both scored low (2/2/2 and 2/1/1): each is an *arm-level* stopping rule the pre-registration never specified (escalation scales reps/tasks within the fixed 4-arm design, not which arms run), each forecloses the required Opus-quality report and/or the Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo gate itself, and each is exactly the fishing-for-a-favorable-cut risk pre-registration exists to prevent (line 27).

## Assumptions
- [checkable-doc] Comment 3 (ISSUE-41.md:65-71) already validated this design against actual code and names it "per Comment 1's stated escalation method" — PM verified by re-reading ISSUE-41.md:33-49 and 65-71 directly.
- [checkable-doc] The Method section's 3-rep base (line 42-43) precedes and is distinct from the Analysis section's escalation clause (line 49) — i.e., escalation is additive to the 3-rep floor, not a replacement starting below it — PM verified via document order and wording ("3 replicates per (task x arm)" under Method vs "add reps/tasks" under Analysis).
- [unverifiable] 3 reps per cell yields a usable (non-degenerate) CI at all — REOPEN-IF: the first escalation checkpoint shows 3-rep CIs are too wide/degenerate to ever resolve for one or more cells, which would mean the starting floor itself needs raising (Advisor 2's O4 reversal-crux), not just the escalation increment.

## Rejected alternatives
- O1 (full grid, no checkpoint) — matches the 3-rep base but runs it "before any analysis," skipping the line-49 escalation checkpoint the pre-registration itself specifies; overspends on already-unambiguous cells.
- O2 (pilot Tiered vs Sonnet-solo only, stop if decidable) — an unregistered arm-level stopping rule; forecloses Haiku-solo/Opus-solo entirely and the mandatory Opus-quality "real prize" report (line 51); the fishing risk pre-registration exists to prevent (line 27).
- O3 (pilot Opus-solo vs Sonnet-solo first) — never runs the Tiered arm at all, so it cannot resolve the actual pre-registered decision gate (Tiered vs Sonnet-solo, line 51); answers an adjacent question, not the one asked.

## Revisit triggers
- First escalation checkpoint shows 3-rep CIs are degenerately wide across multiple cells -> raise the starting-rep floor above 3 rather than continuing incremental escalation (Advisor 2's reversal premise).
- Owner formally amends the pre-registration to decouple the Opus-quality "real prize" report from being a required deliverable of this run -> O2-style arm gating becomes worth re-scoring for cost, per Advisor 1's O2 reversal premise.
